title: Needle-felted landscape panel
subtitle: A layered pictorial panel: sky, hillside, and foreground in needle-felted wool
excerpt: Build a small landscape panel on a wet-felted background: a graduated sky in two blues, a green hill line, and a textured foreground. The technique uses flat layers of coloured roving needled in sequence from the furthest plane to the nearest, producing depth through overlapping colour.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted pictorial panels are a well-established domestic craft, particularly in the UK where the tradition draws on the Yorkshire and Lake District landscape paintings of the Arts and Crafts period. The layered-planes composition method is observation-led.

content.text: This panel measures approximately 25 x 20 cm finished. The
is a wet-felted panel in pale blue-grey, made first and dried before needle-felting begins. The landscape is built as
laid from the most distant plane (sky) to the nearest (foreground), so each plane overlaps slightly over the one behind it, producing visual depth.

attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out into a foam mat large enough to cover the full work surface.
attrs.title: Needle safety

items.name: Merino roving, two shades of sky blue (sky layers)
Merino roving, pale gold or straw (foreground field)
Merino roving, grey-brown (tree or dry-stone wall detail)
Foam felting mat, 30 x 25 cm or larger
Wet-felting kit for the background panel

content.text: Follow the wet-felting-a-flat-panel technique to make a 30 x 24 cm pale blue-grey panel (it will shrink to about 25 x 20 cm when finished and dry). Allow the panel to dry completely before needle-felting on top. A damp background panel makes needle-felting difficult because the fibres are softer and slippery.

Place the dry background panel on the foam mat. Pull thin wisps of pale sky-blue roving and lay them across the top third of the panel in loose horizontal sweeps. Needle them in with the size 38 needle, working from the upper edge downward. Then lay a slightly deeper blue over the lower sky zone and blend the two blues where they overlap by working the needle along the boundary line. The sky should read as graduated from pale at the top to deeper at the horizon.

Lay a band of dark green roving across the panel at roughly the two-thirds-down mark, with an undulating top edge to suggest a hill. Needle the top edge firmly, stabbing at a slight downward angle to define the hill profile against the sky. Needle the whole band flat. Overlap the green slightly over the lower sky zone so the hill reads as sitting in front of the sky plane.

Lay a band of lighter green below the dark hill, overlapping its lower edge slightly. Then lay pale gold or straw-coloured roving across the lower quarter of the panel for the foreground field. Each successive plane overlaps slightly over the one above, reinforcing the sense of depth. Add any detail elements: a row of grey-brown stitches needled in for a dry-stone wall, or a small dark-green mass for a tree against the sky.

Work the fine size 40 needle shallowly across the whole panel surface to smooth any standing fibres and sharpen the colour boundaries. The panel can be mounted on card, stretched over a small wooden frame, or framed directly behind glass.

attrs.intro: Two problems specific to pictorial needle felting.

items.fix: Needle each colour layer flat before starting on the next. Define the boundary by needling along it with the fine needle at a slight angle after both layers are in place.
items.cause: Too much needle pressure at the boundary before each layer is independently secured.
items.symptom: Colour boundaries look blurred and muddy rather than clean

items.fix: Work the needle deeper (to the full depth of the needle) in areas where the roving is lifting. If the background panel feels soft, needle it more before adding colour.
items.cause: The background panel was too soft when you started. Or the stabs were too shallow to lock the colour into the background.
items.symptom: Roving is pulling up from the background between colour patches

attrs.heading: Common problems
